originalTitle: Police block thousands of 'cockroach' protesters marching on India's parliament
summary: Some protesters demanding education reforms said they were injured in clashes with police who banned the march.
content:
Tens of thousands of people attended Monday's protests

Thousands of supporters of the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP), a youth-led protest movement seeking education and employment reforms, defied a police ban and tried to march on parliament to demand India's education minister resign.

Police wielding canes used tear gas to disperse crowds in central Delhi. Several demonstrators said they were injured in the cane charges. Police did not comment. Some in the crowd threw stones, reports said.

The march followed weeks of protests over alleged exam paper leaks and accusations of a wider education system crisis.

The movement has broadened into one of the most visible expressions of public dissent against Prime Minister Narendra Modi in recent years.

Clashes were reported between the police and protesters in some places

It gathered fresh momentum over the weekend after police forcibly moved activist Sonam Wangchuk into hospital on the 21st day of a hunger strike in support of the movement. His wife says his fast continues. On Monday, Wangchuk appealed to doctors to discharge him so he could join the march but was not allowed.

The movement takes its name from a remark by India's chief justice, who drew criticism after comparing sections of unemployed young people to "cockroaches" during a court hearing.

Although police had made clear protesters would not be given permission to march to parliament, tens of thousands of students, parents, teachers and professionals gathered from early morning at Jantar Mantar, a 300-year-old observatory in central Delhi, and remained there despite heavy rain.

It remains unclear whether the CJP will call off its protest or try to continue the sit-in. By late evening police had completely dismantled the protest site.

The contrast between it and the security outside was striking. Young people draped in Indian flags queued for food, painted placards and posed for photographs, while rows of police and paramilitary personnel sealed every road to parliament around them.

Volunteers handed out food and water, patriotic songs blared from loudspeakers and organisers repeatedly urged supporters not to confront police if they were stopped.

The protest march began from Jantar Mantar, where thousands have been camping for weeks

As the march began, protesters spilled onto the streets before being halted at successive barricades. Many sat on the road waving flags and shouting slogans.

Others broke away in smaller groups, trying to reach parliament through narrow side streets.

Inside parliament, meanwhile, the opening day of the monsoon session was repeatedly adjourned as opposition MPs protested over the alleged paper leak and other issues.

As police pushed back groups trying to breach the barricades, the first reports of injuries began to emerge.

Some protesters returned to Jantar Mantar bleeding from the head or limping, while others were carried back by fellow demonstrators. Authorities also suspended mobile internet services in parts of central Delhi, leaving many without reliable access to messaging apps or social media.

There was heavy police deployment, with barricades and personnel stopping protesters from marching

"We've treated at least 10 people," volunteer doctor Gaurish Batra told the BBC while bandaging the head of a man whose shirt collar was soaked in blood. A woman with a suspected broken leg was also brought in for treatment.

Both alleged they had been injured in police baton charges. The BBC has contacted Delhi Police for comment.

By early afternoon, two CJP representatives met Health Minister JP Nadda after waiting outside his residence for about two hours.

According to CJP spokesperson Saurav Das, they submitted a letter setting out the group's demands. Das said Nadda assured them he would raise the matter within the government.

Shortly after, Nadda posted on X, urging protesters to end their demonstration and help restore "normalcy" in the capital.

Neither the meeting, nor the statement, did much to ease tensions. Thousands remained at Jantar Mantar, hoping they would eventually be allowed to continue the march.

The protests were led by Abhijeet Dipke, the founder of CJP

When the rain arrived, it barely interrupted the protest. Volunteers stretched blue tarpaulins over small groups as chants continued and soaked demonstrators waited for news from the police barricades.

Among those who had travelled to Delhi was commercial pilot Samrat Khanna, who said he had swapped flying duties to attend the march.

"I felt I had to be here," he said. "I couldn't just watch from the sidelines."

Many others expressed similar feelings. For them, the alleged exam paper leaks had become less the movement's only cause than the catalyst for broader frustrations over jobs, opportunity, transparency and accountability.

The CJP wants India's Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan to resign

Those frustrations are at the heart of the rise of the Cockroach Janta Party , or CJP, founded by Dipke.

Although the chief justice later withdrew his comment, Dipke embraced the label, turning the cockroach into the movement's symbol.

Monday's turnout reflected how quickly the movement has grown since its first protest in June, when it was little more than a fledgling online campaign. Since then, it has spread to cities across India, drawing support from students, professionals, activists and opposition politicians.

By evening, however, the mood had shifted.

Police in riot gear entered Jantar Mantar and announced over loudspeakers that protesters no longer had permission to remain. Officers then began pushing people back, prompting some demonstrators to flee while others linked arms and refused to leave.

There was also brief confusion over Dipke's whereabouts after the CJP initially said he had been detained. The group later clarified that police had attempted to take him into custody but protesters had prevented it. Delhi Police later denied the claims, calling th
chineseTitle: 警方阻止数千名“蟑螂”抗议者向印度议会游行
chineseSummary: 一些要求教育改革的抗议者表示，他们在与禁止游行的警察发生冲突时受伤。
chineseContent:
数万人参加周一的抗议活动

蟑螂简塔党（CJP）是一个由青年领导的寻求教育和就业改革的抗议运动，其数千名支持者无视警方的禁令，试图在议会游行，要求印度教育部长辞职。

在德里市中心，挥舞着手杖的警察使用催泪瓦斯驱散人群。几名示威者表示，他们在藤杖袭击中受伤。警方没有发表评论。报道称，人群中有些人投掷石块。

这次游行是在针对所谓试卷泄露和对更广泛的教育系统危机的指控进行了数周的抗议活动之后进行的。

近年来，该运动已扩大为公众对纳伦德拉·莫迪总理最明显的异议表达方式之一。

据报道，部分地方警察与抗议者发生冲突

在抗议支持该运动的活动人士索南·旺楚克 (Sonam Wangchuk) 第 21 天，警方强行将其送入医院后，该活动在周末聚集了新的势头。他的妻子说他的禁食仍在继续。周一，旺楚克请求医生让他出院，以便他可以参加游行，但遭到拒绝。

该运动得名于印度首席大法官的一句话，他在法庭听证会上将部分失业年轻人比作“蟑螂”后招致批评。

尽管警方明确表示不会允许抗议者游行到议会，但数万名学生、家长、教师和专业人士从清晨起就聚集在德里市中心一座拥有 300 年历史的简塔·曼塔天文台，尽管下着大雨，他们仍留在那里。

目前尚不清楚 CJP 是否会取消抗议活动或尝试继续静坐。到傍晚时分，警方已彻底拆除了抗议场地。

它与外面的安全状况形成鲜明对比。披着印度国旗的年轻人排队领取食物、画标语牌并摆好姿势拍照，一排排警察和准军事人员封锁了周围通往议会的每条道路。

志愿者分发食物和水，扩音器里播放着爱国歌曲，组织者一再敦促支持者在被拦截时不要与警察对抗。

抗议游行从简塔·曼塔天文台开始，数千人已经在那里露营了数周

游行开始后，抗议者涌上街道，然后被连续的路障拦住。许多人坐在路上挥舞旗帜，高喊口号。

其他人则分成较小的群体，试图通过狭窄的小巷到达议会。

与此同时，在议会内部，由于反对派议员对所谓的文件泄露和其他问题提出抗议，季风会议的开幕日一再推迟。

当警察击退试图冲破路障的团体时，第一批受伤报告开始出现。

一些抗议者返回简塔·曼塔天文台时头部流血或一瘸一拐，而其他抗议者则被其他示威者抬了回来。当局还暂停了德里市中心部分地区的移动互联网服务，导致许多人无法可靠地访问消息应用程序或社交媒体。

部署了大量警察，设置路障和人员阻止抗议者游行

“我们已经治疗了至少 10 个人，”志愿者医生高里什·巴特拉 (Gaurish Batra) 一边给一名衬衫领子浸满血的男子的头部包扎，一边告诉 BBC。一名疑似腿部骨折的妇女也被带去接受治疗。

两人都声称自己在警察警棍袭击中受伤。 BBC 已联系德里警方征求意见。

下午早些时候，两名 CJP 代表在卫生部长 JP Nadda 的住所外等了大约两个小时后会见了他。

据 CJP 发言人 Saurav Das 称，他们提交了一封信函，阐述了该组织的要求。达斯说，纳达向他们保证，他将在政府内部提出此事。

不久之后，纳达在 X 上发帖，敦促抗议者结束示威，帮助首都恢复“正常”。

无论是会议还是声明都没有对缓解紧张局势起到多大作用。数千人留在简塔·曼塔天文台，希望他们最终能被允许继续游行。

抗议活动由 CJP 创始人阿比吉特·迪普克 (Abhijeet Dipke) 领导

当雨来临时，抗议活动几乎没有中断。志愿者们在一小群人身上铺上蓝色防水油布，口号仍在继续，浑身湿透的示威者等待警察路障传来的消息。

前往德里的人中有商业飞行员萨姆拉特·卡纳(Samrat Khanna)，他说他已经调换了飞行职责来参加游行。

“我觉得我必须来这里，”他说。 “我不能只是在一旁观看。”

许多其他人也表达了类似的感受。对他们来说，所谓的试卷泄露事件已不再是这场运动的唯一原因，而是引发对就业、机会、透明度和问责制的更广泛挫败感的催化剂。

CJP 希望印度教育部长达门德拉·普拉丹 (Dharmendra Pradhan) 辞职

这些挫折是迪普克创立的蟑螂简塔党（CJP）崛起的核心。

尽管首席大法官后来撤回了他的评论，但迪普克接受了这个标签，将蟑螂变成了该运动的象征。

周一的投票人数反映出该运动自 6 月份首次抗议以来发展速度有多快，当时这只不过是一场刚刚起步的在线运动。从那时起，它已经蔓延到印度各地的城市，得到了学生、专业人士、活动人士和反对派政客的支持。

然而到了晚上，气氛就发生了转变。

身穿防暴装备的警察进入简塔·曼塔天文台，并通过扩音器宣布抗议者不再获准留下。随后，警察开始将人们驱赶，导致一些示威者逃跑，而另一些人则手挽着手拒绝离开。

在 CJP 最初表示迪普克已被拘留后，人们对他的下落也存在短暂的困惑。该组织后来澄清说，警方曾试图拘留他，但抗议者阻止了这一行动。德里警方后来否认了这一说法，称
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
